names.japanese_name: フェロシアン化カリウム

natural_occurrence.description: Potassium ferrocyanide does not occur naturally. It is synthetically manufactured, primarily for use in wine making and salt products.

manufacturing.description: Produced similarly to sodium ferrocyanide, but using potassium compounds. The hexacyanoferrate complex is extremely stable with cyanide groups tightly coordinated to the iron center.

other_uses.industrial: Wine fining, case hardening of steel, photography, pigment production.
other_uses.household: Present in some table salt products.

eu.conditions: Restricted to use in salt and salt substitutes, and wine. Maximum levels strictly enforced.

usa.notes: Limited to 13 ppm in table salt.

japan.japanese_regulation_name: フェロシアン化カリウム

safety_evaluations.jecfa_adi: 0-0.025 mg/kg bw/day (as ferrocyanide ion, for all ferrocyanides)
safety_evaluations.efsa_adi: 0.03 mg/kg bw/day (as ferrocyanide ion, group ADI for all ferrocyanides)

regulatory_history.event: EFSA re-evaluated all ferrocyanides as a group and confirmed safety at current use levels.

commonly_found_in: Wine (fining agent to remove excess iron and prevent cloudiness)
fun_facts: Like E535, contains CYANIDE but is completely SAFE - cyanide locked in stable iron complex
Widely used in winemaking to remove iron that could cause blue-black cloudiness (iron casse)
The potassium version is preferred in wine because it doesn't add sodium
Forms beautiful yellow crystals - hence the name 'yellow prussiate of potash'
Same safety profile as sodium ferrocyanide - the cyanide cannot be released under normal conditions
Has been used in wine fining for over a century with excellent safety record
Works by binding to iron ions and precipitating them out of wine or preventing crystal formation in salt
